English: red-faced / flushed
Note:
Often from embarrassment, anger, or argument.
面 (face) 红 (red) 耳 (ear) 赤 (flushed)

English: wrapped in white / covered in snow
Note:
Poetic description of a snowy white landscape.
银 (silver) 装 (dress) 素 (white) 裹 (wrap)

English: after bitterness comes sweetness
Note:
Hardship eventually leads to good results.
苦 (bitter) 尽 (end) 甘 (sweet) 来 (come)

English: have you been well since we last met?
Note:
Old-fashioned/literary greeting. Good for classical flavor, not normal daily greeting.
别 (part) 来 (since) 无 (no) 恙 (illness)

English: through and through / complete
Note:
Means completely or downright, often before a negative noun/adjective.
彻 (through) 头 (head) 尾 (tail)

English: brought to perfection
Note:
Describes a skill that has reached a very high level.
炉 (furnace) 火 (fire) 纯 (pure) 青 (blue-green)

English: courtesy should be reciprocated
Note:
Used for social reciprocity: favors, gifts, respect, politeness.
礼 (courtesy) 尚 (esteem) 往 (go) 来 (come)

English: family happiness
Note:
Literary phrase for joy from family relationships.
天 (heaven) 伦 (relations) 之 (of) 乐 (joy)

English: to rack one’s brains
Note:
Literally to squeeze out all the brain juice; used when thinking very hard.
绞 (wring) 尽 (exhaust) 脑 (brain) 汁 (juice)

English: completely different
Note:
Useful idiom-like phrase; fairly practical.
截 (cut) 然 (so) 不 (not) 同 (same)

English: like-minded
Note:
Used for people who share goals, ideals, or values.
志 (aspiration) 同 (same) 道 (path) 合 (match)

English: deep affection and friendship
Note:
Formal/literary expression for a strong bond.
深 (deep) 情 (feeling) 厚 (thick) 谊 (friendship)

English: a true soulmate is hard to find
Note:
Literary phrase about finding someone who truly understands you.
知 (know) 音 (tone) 难 (hard) 觅 (seek)

English: to add fuel to the fire
Note:
Used when making an already bad situation worse.
火 (fire) 上 (on) 浇 (pour) 油 (oil)

English: burning with anger / seething
Note:
Literary-sounding expression for intense anger.
怒 (anger) 火 (fire) 中 (within) 烧 (burn)

Pinyin: chī yí qiàn, zhǎng yí zhì
English: learn from a setback
Note:
Proverb: one fall into a pit, one gain in wisdom.
吃 (eat) 一 (one) 堑 (pit) 长 (gain) 智 (wisdom)

English: practice makes perfect
Note:
Very useful proverb/idiom.
熟 (familiar) 能 (can) 生 (produce) 巧 (skill)

English: great talent matures late
Note:
Used for people who succeed later after long development.
大 (great) 器 (vessel) 晚 (late) 成 (achieve)

English: hardship produces talent
Note:
Proverb-like motivational saying.
逆 (adverse) 境 (situation) 出 (produce) 人 (person) 才 (talent)

English: constant effort brings sure success
Note:
Proverb: when the work is done, success follows naturally.
功 (effort) 到 (arrive) 自 (self) 然 (so) 成 (succeed)

Pinyin: bù pà màn, jiù pà zhàn
English: better slow progress than standing still
Note:
Proverb encouraging steady progress.
不 (not) 怕 (fear) 慢 (slow) 就 (only) 站 (stand still)

English: skilled in both pen and sword
Note:
Classical/literary praise for someone capable in both intellectual and martial areas.
文 (literary) 武 (martial) 双 (both) 全 (complete)

Hanzi: 谦虚使人进步，骄傲使人落后
Pinyin: qiānxū shǐ rén jìnbù, jiāo'ào shǐ rén luòhòu
English: modesty helps one progress; pride makes one fall behind
Note:
Well-known saying associated with self-improvement and humility.
谦 (modest) 虚 (humble) 使 (cause) 人 (person) 进 (advance) 步 (step) 骄 (proud) 傲 (arrogant) 落 (fall) 后 (behind)

Pinyin: jūn jūn, chén chén, fù fù, zǐ zǐ
English: rulers should act as rulers, ministers as ministers, fathers as fathers, sons as sons
Note:
Classical Confucian phrase about proper social roles.
君 (ruler) 臣 (minister) 父 (father) 子 (son)

Pinyin: yǒu zhì zhě shì jìng chéng
English: where there is a will, there is a way
Note:
Common motivational proverb.
有 (have) 志 (will) 者 (one who) 事 (matter) 竟 (ultimately) 成 (succeed)

Hanzi: 舍得一身剐，敢把皇帝拉下马
Pinyin: shěde yìshēn guǎ, gǎn bǎ huángdì lā xià mǎ
English: one who fears not being cut to pieces dares to unhorse the emperor
Note:
Bold proverb about daring to challenge power despite great risk.
舍 (give up) 得 (willing) 一 (one) 身 (body) 剐 (cut to pieces) 敢 (dare) 把 (take) 皇 (imperial) 帝 (emperor) 拉 (pull) 下 (down) 马 (horse)

English: cross the river by feeling the stones
Note:
Proceed cautiously through trial and error.
摸 (feel) 着 (-ing) 石 (stone) 头 (suffix) 过 (cross) 河 (river)

English: to worry unnecessarily
Note:
Literally a man of Qi worrying the sky will fall; imaginary/unnecessary anxiety.
杞 (Qi state) 人 (man) 忧 (worry) 天 (sky)

English: draw a snake and add feet / overdo it
Note:
From a fable — adding superfluous detail ruins the work. Used when extra effort backfires.
画 (draw) 蛇 (snake) 添 (add) 足 (foot)

English: wait by the stump for a rabbit
Note:
Fable: farmer saw one rabbit hit a stump, then quit farming to wait. Means passive reliance on luck.
守 (guard) 株 (stump) 待 (wait) 兔 (rabbit)

English: old frontier-man lost his horse / blessing in disguise
Note:
Often paired with 焉知非福 — bad luck may be good in disguise.
塞 (frontier) 翁 (old man) 失 (lose) 马 (horse)

English: one stroke, two gains / kill two birds
Note:
Very practical. Same idea as English "two birds, one stone".
一 (one) 举 (act) 两 (two) 得 (gain)

English: when in Rome, do as the Romans
Note:
Literally "enter village, follow customs". Daily-life usable.
入 (enter) 乡 (village) 随 (follow) 俗 (custom)

English: self-contradictory
Note:
From a tale of a seller hawking an unstoppable spear and an unbreakable shield.
自 (self) 相 (mutually) 矛 (spear) 盾 (shield)

English: quench thirst by gazing at plums / false hope
Note:
Cao Cao tricked thirsty troops by promising plums ahead. Means consoling with imagination.
望 (gaze) 梅 (plum) 止 (stop) 渴 (thirst)

English: give up halfway
Note:
Common warning against quitting partway through.
半 (half) 途 (way) 而 (and) 废 (abandon)

English: dripping water pierces stone / persistence wins
Note:
Classic image for slow, steady effort beating brute force.
水 (water) 滴 (drip) 石 (stone) 穿 (pierce)

English: frog at the bottom of a well / narrow worldview
Note:
Insult-ish: someone whose view of the world is tiny.
井 (well) 底 (bottom) 之 (of) 蛙 (frog)

Pinyin: dīshuǐ zhī ēn, dāng yǒngquán xiāng bào
English: repay a drop of kindness with a gushing spring
Note:
Classical gratitude proverb. Strong literary tone.
滴 (drip) 水 (water) 之 (of) 恩 (kindness) 当 (should) 涌 (gush) 泉 (spring) 相 (mutually) 报 (repay)

English: know yourself and your enemy — never lose
Note:
From Sunzi Art of War. Still quoted in modern speech.
知 (know) 己 (self) 彼 (other) 百 (hundred) 战 (battle) 不 (not) 殆 (peril)
